Party_Details.Name: De Cruz Andrea Heidi

Facts.Text: Andrea De Cruz graduated with a degree in Clinical Psychology from the University of San Francisco in 1997.
After returning to Singapore in 1998, Andrea De Cruz worked as a Clinical Psychologist in a clinic for about one year.
After her work as a Clinical Psychologist, Andrea De Cruz joined Television Corporation of Singapore, the predecessor of MediaCorp, and worked there primarily as a television host and actress.
In 2001, Andrea De Cruz was cast in the Chinese drama series 'No Problem', which was a significant role for her in the Chinese language entertainment market.
Before her illness, Andrea De Cruz maintained a hectic lifestyle that included frequent gym attendance and participation in various exercise classes.
By 2001, Andrea De Cruz was engaged to Pierre Png, and they regularly engaged in outdoor activities together such as going to the beach, cycling, swimming and motorcycle rides.
In November 2001, Andrea De Cruz underwent an operation to remove a lump from her left breast and was advised thereafter to reduce physical exertion.
Sometime in December 2001, during filming of 'No Problem', Andrea De Cruz became acquainted with the fifth defendant, Rayson Tan Tai Ming, and his wife, Chen Liping, both MediaCorp artistes.
During filming of 'No Problem' in December 2001, Andrea De Cruz spent substantial time with the fifth defendant as she was cast as his wife in the series.
Around December 2001, Andrea De Cruz saw television commercials for Slim 10 produced by the fourth defendant, featuring Chen Liping with 'before and after' images showing weight loss.

The Slim 10 television commercials viewed by Andrea De Cruz were approximately 30 seconds long in both Chinese and English versions and described the pills as completely herbal, featuring the fourth defendant as distributor.
Around late 2001, Andrea De Cruz saw life-sized cardboard cutouts and large posters of Chen Liping advertising Slim 10 at the fourth defendant's retail outlet in Beach Road.

By late 2001, Andrea De Cruz and her family were familiar with the fourth defendant as a large company from which they had previously purchased products, including products advertised in the United States.
Andrea De Cruz continued to see Slim 10 advertisements on various television channels in Singapore up to late March 2002.

During a filming session for 'No Problem' in December 2001, Andrea De Cruz told the fifth defendant that she had seen the Slim 10 advertisements and complimented him on his wife's weight loss; he responded that the pills worked.

In the same December 2001 conversation, Andrea De Cruz told the fifth defendant she had reduced gym attendance after her November 2001 breast operation, was gaining weight due to irregular filming meal times, and was concerned about this; the fifth defendant suggested she take Slim 10 to help maintain her weight.

A few days after the December 2001 conversation, the fifth defendant asked Andrea De Cruz at the film studio if she wanted Slim 10 pills he had in his car and offered them at $130 per bottle of 120 capsules, below the $149.90 retail price.
On or about 11 January 2002, Andrea De Cruz bought two sets of 120 Slim 10 capsules from the fifth defendant for $260 in cash.
On or about 11 January 2002, the fifth defendant handed Andrea De Cruz two clear, unmarked test tubes containing Slim 10 capsules.

Before taking delivery of the first batch of Slim 10 capsules around 11 January 2002, Andrea De Cruz asked the fifth defendant about possible side effects and was told she might experience insomnia, feeling cold, calf pain, and that the pills were described as natural and containing only Chinese herbs.

During filming of 'No Problem' from 3 December 2001 to 11 February 2002, Andrea De Cruz delayed starting Slim 10 consumption because she was concerned that insomnia might affect her performance in her first major Chinese role.
In late January or early February 2002, after completion of filming for 'No Problem', Andrea De Cruz began consuming Slim 10 capsules.
When Andrea De Cruz first started taking Slim 10 in late January or early February 2002, she followed the recommended dosage of four capsules three times a day, totaling 12 capsules daily.
After starting Slim 10, Andrea De Cruz experienced some of the side effects described by the fifth defendant, including insomnia, feeling cold, calf discomfort, and palpitations.
After some time on Slim 10, Andrea De Cruz reduced her daily intake to a pattern of four capsules in the morning, four in the afternoon, and two in the evening to lessen sleep disruption.
On 12 February 2002, Andrea De Cruz sent an SMS to the fifth defendant stating: 'Hi, wishing u, Li Ping & baby gd health & prosperity this CNY! Luv Andrea'.

On 15 February 2002, Andrea De Cruz sent an SMS to the fifth defendant stating: 'Gd morning Mr Extremly Naughty, did Cai Shen bring u a lot of wealth this CNY? Anyway i hv run out of skinny pills. Will u be around mcs 2day?'.
On or about 18 February 2002, Andrea De Cruz paid the fifth defendant $260 by cheque dated 18 February 2002 for a second batch of Slim 10 capsules.

Around 18 February 2002, the fifth defendant provided Andrea De Cruz with a second batch of Slim 10 comprising two sets of 120 capsules each, packed in aluminium foil, which he said had been specially packed by the Slim 10 boss because he kept the pills in his car.
In early to mid March 2002, after finishing the first batch of Slim 10, Andrea De Cruz started consuming the second batch of Slim 10 capsules.

On 29 March 2002, Andrea De Cruz travelled to Bangkok to visit her fiancé, bringing with her remaining Slim 10 capsules from the second batch and consuming them there according to her usual dosage pattern.
On 11 April 2002, Andrea De Cruz returned to Singapore from Bangkok with no remaining Slim 10 capsules.

After returning to Singapore on 11 April 2002, Andrea De Cruz sent an SMS to the fifth defendant requesting more Slim 10 pills, although the exact wording of the message was not recalled and the message was not retrieved.

Between 12 and 15 April 2002, the fifth defendant left two bottles of Slim 10 in Andrea De Cruz's pigeonhole at MediaCorp, which she collected; she did not make payment for this third batch before her subsequent hospitalisation.
On Sunday, 14 April 2002, during lunch after church, Andrea De Cruz's sister observed that Andrea's eyes appeared very yellow and jaundiced, and the family decided she should seek medical attention.

On 15 April 2002, Andrea De Cruz visited her regular general practitioner with Pierre Png, was told her liver was inflamed and her eyes were very jaundiced, and was advised to be admitted to hospital immediately.
On 15 April 2002, after packing at home, Andrea De Cruz went to Changi General Hospital where liver inflammation was confirmed and she was hospitalised.
In the early hours of 16 April 2002, due to lack of single-bed rooms at Changi General Hospital, Andrea De Cruz was transferred to Mount Alvernia Hospital.
From 16 April 2002, Dr Chia Siew Cheng was the doctor in charge of Andrea De Cruz at Mount Alvernia Hospital, where she underwent multiple medical tests.
During her stay at Mount Alvernia Hospital, Andrea De Cruz experienced daily gum bleeding when brushing her teeth, persistent bleeding due to clotting issues, and bruises from repeated blood draws.
Sometime in the second week of her hospitalisation at Mount Alvernia Hospital, Andrea De Cruz underwent a liver biopsy, after which she experienced severe pain and bodily shaking and trembling.
On 3 May 2002, Dr Dede Selamat Sutedja from the National University Hospital liver team examined Andrea De Cruz at Mount Alvernia Hospital and prescribed drugs which she vomited after taking.
On 4 May 2002, Andrea De Cruz was transferred from Mount Alvernia Hospital to the National University Hospital.

On 5 May 2002, at National University Hospital, Andrea De Cruz became encephalopathic, was unable to recognise people, spoke incoherently, and was moved to the Intensive Care Unit for immediate liver dialysis.
By 5 May 2002, potential family donors for Andrea De Cruz were assessed and found unsuitable for a living donor liver transplant under then-existing policy limiting donors to relatives.
By early May 2002, the Ministry of Health granted special permission for an unrelated living donor liver transplant, allowing Andrea De Cruz to receive a liver donation from her fiancé, Pierre Png.

On 7 May 2002, Andrea De Cruz's family arranged for her transfer from National University Hospital to Gleneagles Hospital so that a surgeon experienced in living donor liver transplants, Dr Tan Kai Chah, could operate.
At Gleneagles Hospital on 7 May 2002, Andrea De Cruz was diagnosed with a thyroid problem and given medication to control it, which delayed the liver transplant operation.
On the night of 7 May 2002, a living donor liver transplant operation lasting about twelve hours was performed on Andrea De Cruz at Gleneagles Hospital, using a liver donation from Pierre Png.
Approximately two weeks after the liver transplant in May 2002, Andrea De Cruz was discharged from Gleneagles Hospital.

At the time of the press conference on her discharge, Andrea De Cruz did not publicly state that she had taken Slim 10, following advice from her brother-in-law and her manager to avoid attributing her condition to the pills.

By the time of her discharge in May 2002, Andrea De Cruz had incurred medical expenses for her treatment and liver transplant, some of which were covered by insurance, and she also paid medical costs for Pierre Png.

Following her transplant, Andrea De Cruz was placed on lifelong immuno-suppressant medication to prevent liver rejection, resulting in a weakened immune system and increased susceptibility to infections.
After the transplant, Andrea De Cruz was advised to avoid direct sunlight, to use sunscreen of at least SPF 15 even on cloudy days, and to cover up when outdoors for extended periods.
Post-transplant, Andrea De Cruz was advised to avoid seafood, raw food, chocolates, and oily food due to removal of her gall bladder and her medical condition.
Following her transplant, Andrea De Cruz experienced easy fatigue, breathlessness with exertion, frequent monthly illnesses, migraines, body aches, and a weakened bladder causing interrupted sleep.
After her transplant, Andrea De Cruz was advised that pregnancy would carry risks due to the need to change immuno-suppressant medication, with potential adverse effects on her liver or a foetus.

Post-illness, Andrea De Cruz's manager advised her not to appear publicly as someone who was ill, to avoid loss of work opportunities, leading her to maintain a bubbly public persona despite ongoing pain and depression.

Due to her hospitalisation and medical condition in 2002, Andrea De Cruz missed the second season of 'All Change' and lost hosting and sponsorship engagements including a Swarovski event, the Sing Singapore launch, the National Day Parade 2002, and a L’Oreal Helena Rubinstein sponsorship, with an estimated income loss of about $14,500.
Although Andrea De Cruz continued to receive her salary from MediaCorp after her illness, she faced a substantial reduction in income from external engagements due to her medical condition.
Following her liver transplant, Andrea De Cruz became effectively uninsurable for future medical coverage, creating potential financial difficulty if she required further operations.
Andrea De Cruz and Pierre Png tentatively set their wedding date for 18 October 2003, although she limited her involvement in preparations due to uncertainty about her health.

After Andrea De Cruz was transferred to National University Hospital, Kevin Pereira informed the NUH liver team about Slim 10, and the doctors indicated that, based on their experience, if she had taken Slim 10 there was a high chance it was related to her condition.
Other MediaCorp personnel, including Andrea De Cruz's manager, two producers, a fellow cast member, and a soundman, obtained Slim 10 through the fifth defendant.

A letter dated 12 February 2003 from Nutricia, USA, stated that Spirulina marketed in Singapore by GNC, which Andrea De Cruz consumed, was grown in controlled freshwater ponds and that each batch of raw material was screened for microcystins and met applicable US food law requirements.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the consumption of Slim 10 slimming capsules obtained through the fifth defendant and imported and distributed by the corporate defendants was a factual cause of the plaintiff's acute liver failure and subsequent need for a liver transplant, and whether each defendant's pre-dispute conduct in manufacturing, importing, distributing, marketing, or supplying the capsules is associated with that outcome.
Party_Details.Rule: In negligence and product-related claims, liability generally depends on whether the defendant's conduct in manufacturing, importing, distributing, marketing, or supplying a product that is consumed or used by the plaintiff is a factual cause of the plaintiff's injury, typically assessed by medical and scientific evidence on causation and by examining the temporal relationship between product use and onset of injury.
Party_Details.Application: The plaintiff was a healthy, working MediaCorp artiste who began consuming Slim 10 capsules in late January or early February 2002 after exposure to extensive advertising and personal assurances about the product's herbal nature and safety. She obtained three batches of Slim 10 through the fifth defendant, paying for the first two batches and collecting the third shortly before her illness. Her consumption of Slim 10 occurred in the weeks immediately preceding the onset of liver inflammation and jaundice observed on 14–15 April 2002. During this period, she was also taking Spirulina and Pycnogenol, which she had consumed regularly for about two and a half years without prior liver issues. After her return from Bangkok on 11 April 2002, she had no remaining Slim 10 capsules and soon thereafter developed signs of liver dysfunction, leading to hospitalisation, transfer between hospitals, liver dialysis, and a living donor liver transplant on 7 May 2002. Multiple medical specialists investigated potential causes, considering her supplement use, travel, and other exposures. The temporal sequence shows that her period of Slim 10 ingestion ended within weeks of the onset of liver derangement, while her use of other supplements had been longstanding without earlier liver problems. The corporate defendants were involved in manufacturing, importing, testing, licensing, distributing, and aggressively marketing Slim 10, and the fifth defendant acted as a conduit through whom the plaintiff obtained unmarked Slim 10 capsules at a discount. These pre-dispute events collectively frame the factual context in which the plaintiff's liver failure and transplant occurred.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the pills consumed by the plaintiff were Slim 10, the period of consumption, and whether Slim 10 caused her liver failure. It concluded that the unmarked capsules supplied through the fifth defendant were Slim 10, based on the supply chain from the importer, the distributor's knowledge of unmarked samples, and the absence of other slimming products imported by the second defendant. The judge preferred the fifth defendant's evidence on dates of supply and the plaintiff's SMS and cheque records, fixing consumption between late January or early February 2002 and no later than 29 March 2002. Applying medical literature on drug-induced hepatotoxicity, particularly Zimmerman on Hepatotoxicity, the court considered the 5–90 day window between ingestion and injury and the 15–35 day window between cessation and onset of injury. It found that even the worst-case scenario for the plaintiff fell within these ranges, supporting a causal link between Slim 10 and the liver injury. Expert evidence from Dr Dede and Dr Tan Kai Chah supported a diagnosis of drug-induced liver failure by exclusion, with Slim 10 as the only new medication containing hepatotoxic substances such as N-nitrosofenfluramine and nicotinamide. The court evaluated contrary expert opinions from Dr Joyce and Dr Wendon, who suggested only a possible or unlikely association, and considered alternative causes such as Spirulina contamination, seafood toxins, and sero-negative hepatitis. It ultimately rejected these alternatives as less consistent with the timing and evidence, and accepted that Slim 10 was the likely cause of the plaintiff's acute liver failure. The court further examined the roles of the importer, distributor, and the fifth defendant. It found that the second defendant failed to keep proper import records, imported unlabelled capsules marked as gifts without customs declarations, and did not provide batch-by-batch declarations of safety or adequate poison testing despite its undertaking to the HSA. The fourth defendant relied on the HSA's product listing and the importer's assurances, but promoted Slim 10 aggressively, used a celebrity spokesperson, and participated in drafting an appeal letter to resist recall despite escalating complaints. The fifth defendant, although describing his actions as doing favours, obtained large quantities of Slim 10 at a discount, supplied them in unmarked containers to colleagues including the plaintiff, and received payments, which the court treated as commercial dealings rather than mere social favours. On these findings, the court held the relevant defendants liable in negligence for the plaintiff's injuries and awarded damages, including general damages for pain and suffering, loss of earning capacity, and future medical expenses, while addressing issues such as mitigation of loss and the appropriate multiplier and multiplicand. The court also considered but rejected arguments that the plaintiff's purchase route or her continued use of other supplements broke the chain of causation.

Party_Details.Name: Guangzhou Yuzhitang Health Products Co Ltd

Facts.Text: In January 2001, the third defendant was introduced in Guangzhou to the Chinese manufacturer of the slimming product later marketed as Slim 10, identified as the first defendant, Guangzhou Yuzhitang Health Products Co Ltd.
In January 2001, the third defendant spent almost one month in Guangzhou discussing with the first defendant his interest in distributing its slimming capsules in Singapore and other markets.

In March 2001, the third defendant ordered 200 bottles of slimming capsules from the first defendant for product testing and consumption; these capsules were blue and white and represented the first batch purchased for testing.

Around April 2001, the third defendant travelled to Guangzhou and signed an agreement on behalf of the second defendant with the first defendant granting the second defendant rights to market the slimming capsules in Singapore, Malaysia, and Thailand.

By 15 August 2001, according to correspondence relayed to the Health Sciences Authority, the first defendant stopped producing an earlier version of its slimming capsules with brown, fragrant, coarse powder and shifted to capsules containing yellowish or orange smooth powder for future production.

In July 2001, the third defendant attended in Guangzhou the launch by the first defendant of a new slimming product named 'yue zhi tang jian fei jiao nang', described as an improved version of the earlier capsules, and received a handbook and brochures about the new product.

On 2 April 2002, while the third defendant was in Guangzhou, he contacted the first defendant about reports of medical problems among Slim 10 consumers; the manufacturer expressed surprise and provided him with an undated newspaper article stating that it had been conferred a Good Manufacturing Practice Standard.

On 20 April 2002, the third defendant travelled again to China to seek more information from the first defendant about the contents of the slimming product, but the manufacturer did not provide further details and the general manager was not available.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the first defendant's manufacture and prior regulatory history of slimming products, including the product supplied to the second defendant and earlier products banned in China, are factually connected to the plaintiff's liver failure after consuming Slim 10.
Party_Details.Rule: A foreign manufacturer of a health product may be held responsible where its product, supplied into a distribution chain, is factually linked to a consumer's injury, and where prior regulatory actions and product composition are assessing product safety and causation.
Party_Details.Application: The first defendant manufactured the slimming capsules that were supplied to the second defendant under a distribution agreement covering Singapore, Malaysia, and Thailand. The capsules were reformulated over time, with changes in powder characteristics and a new product name, and were the source of all Slim 10 capsules imported into Singapore. The plaintiff later learned from public reports that a slimming product of the first defendant had been banned by China's Ministry of Health in January 2000 for containing prohibited substances, and that the first defendant continued selling products associated with health problems in China in 2001. These facts the first defendant controlled the composition and production of the capsules that entered the Singapore market and that there had been prior regulatory concerns about its slimming products, forming part of the factual background to the plaintiff's consumption of Slim 10 and subsequent liver failure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated the first defendant as the originating manufacturer of the capsules marketed as Slim 10 in Singapore. It noted that the first defendant entered into an agreement with the second defendant to supply slimming capsules for distribution in Singapore and the region, and that all Slim 10 capsules originated from the first defendant's factory. The judge considered evidence that China's Ministry of Health had previously banned one of the first defendant's slimming products for containing prohibited substances and that the first defendant continued selling products that led to health problems in China. These matters were used to assess the reliability and track record of the manufacturer and to evaluate whether the Singapore importer and distributor had exercised adequate care in dealing with it. The court concluded that the first defendant's product, as manufactured and supplied, contained undeclared substances including N-nitrosofenfluramine and nicotinamide, and that this product was the one consumed by the plaintiff. On that basis, the manufacturer was held responsible along with the importer and distributor for the plaintiff's injuries.

Party_Details.Name: HealthBiz Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The third defendant used a dormant company, later named HealthBiz Pte Ltd, to conduct the business of importing and selling the slimming product that became known as Slim 10.
In April 2001, Tan Eng Kiat became a shareholder and director of the company that was later renamed HealthBiz Pte Ltd, with the third defendant as the majority shareholder.
On 1 June 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd offered Peter Boo a full-time position as Vice President, while the third defendant served as President.

In March 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd, through the third defendant, arranged for slimming capsules obtained from the first defendant to be sent to DAC Pharmalab Pte Ltd for microbiological and toxic heavy metal testing at Setsco Services Pte Ltd.
In April 2001, DAC Pharmalab provided HealthBiz Pte Ltd with two Setsco test reports on the slimming capsules, and DAC informed the third defendant and Peter Boo that nothing harmful was detected.

Around April 2001, the third defendant signed an agreement on behalf of HealthBiz Pte Ltd with the first defendant granting HealthBiz Pte Ltd rights to market the slimming capsules in Singapore, Malaysia, and Thailand.

In early May 2001, Peter Boo purchased a Health Sciences Authority guide on the control of Chinese Proprietary Medicines and prepared submissions for importer and wholesale dealer licences for HealthBiz Pte Ltd.
On 11 May 2001, the company adopted the name HealthBiz Pte Ltd.

On 1 June 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd submitted to the Health Sciences Authority applications for import and wholesale dealer licences and for product listing of Slim 10, including product information, formula, packaging designs, Setsco test reports, and Chinese regulatory documents.
On 27 June 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd received from the Health Sciences Authority the import and wholesale dealer licences for Slim 10.

In August 2001, the Health Sciences Authority queried HealthBiz Pte Ltd about differences in capsule colour and powder characteristics between the Chinese product and Slim 10; HealthBiz relayed the manufacturer's explanations and sought further testing from Setsco.

In late August 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd requested Setsco to conduct additional tests on the Chinese blue-and-white capsules to assist the Health Sciences Authority in confirming similarity with Slim 10.

On 3 September 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd sent a letter to the Health Sciences Authority addressing concerns about capsule colour and powder, and informed that the manufacturer had stopped producing the earlier brown, fragrant, coarse powder capsules from 15 August 2001, with future production using yellowish or orange smooth powder.

In October 2001, the second defendant got Setsco to do a cholesterol test on Slim 10 because of a suspicion raised that the product might contain an extract from shellfish, and sent the report and further documents from the Chinese manufacturer to HSA.

On 1 November 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd, through Peter Boo, provided the Health Sciences Authority with a letter of undertaking stating that to the best of its ability it would ensure Slim 10 contained no poisons or synthetic substances and would submit toxic heavy metal and microbial contamination test results within two months of each consignment's import.
On 2 November 2001, the Health Sciences Authority approved the product listing for Slim 10 submitted by HealthBiz Pte Ltd.
On 3 November 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd placed its first purchase order with the first defendant for 5,000 bottles of Slim 10.

On 1 March 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd entered into a sole distributor agreement with the fourth defendant, TV Media Pte Ltd, appointing TV Media as sole distributor for Slim 10 and agreeing that HealthBiz would pay the endorsement fee for the chosen spokesperson.

From 16 October 2001 onwards, HealthBiz Pte Ltd supplied Slim 10 capsules in various forms, including unmarked vials and foil packets, to the fifth defendant at discounted prices for distribution to artistes and acquaintances.
HealthBiz Pte Ltd agreed that the distribution agreement with the fourth defendant signed on 1 March 2001 would take effect from 1 December 2001 due to delays in obtaining regulatory approval.
On 30 November 2001, HealthBiz Pte Ltd placed a second order for 5,000 bottles of Slim 10 with the first defendant.
On 4 January 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd ordered an additional 10,000 bottles of Slim 10 from the first defendant.

On 7 January 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd submitted a batch of Slim 10 capsules to Setsco for batch testing, with reports later sent to the Health Sciences Authority indicating batch number L1-2001 and an expiry date of 31 December 2003.
On 1 February 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd ordered another 10,000 sets of Slim 10 from the first defendant.

On 25 February 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd requested Setsco to perform toxic heavy metal, microbial contamination, and physical and chemical characteristics tests on another batch of Slim 10, and on the same day sent samples to the Health Sciences Authority for a general screening for poisons and synthetic drugs.

On 4 March 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd informed the Health Sciences Authority that the Slim 10 sample submitted for testing corresponded to manufacturing details A1-2002, meaning January Lot number 1 produced in 2002, and noted that the capsule powder was brownish.
In early 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd submitted revised wording for Slim 10 box and label text to the Health Sciences Authority, which was approved after amendment.

On 2 April 2002, after a Health Sciences Authority meeting about consumer complaints, HealthBiz Pte Ltd wrote to the Authority agreeing to a voluntary two-week suspension of Slim 10 distribution and a temporary recall, and pledged cooperation.

On 3 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd prepared an appeal letter to the Health Sciences Authority seeking reconsideration of the voluntary recall and asking for time to obtain investigation results from a Chinese medicine technology centre.

On 4 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd sent one box of 120 Slim 10 capsules to the Health Sciences Authority for testing for thyroxine, fenfluramine, and dexfenfluramine, but was informed that the Authority could not conduct the test due to a conflict of interest.
On 8 April 2002, following instructions from HealthBiz Pte Ltd, the fourth defendant began recalling Slim 10 batches delivered prior to 12 March 2002 that bore the old dosage instructions.

On 15 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd received a letter from the Health Sciences Authority directing immediate suspension of all Slim 10 sales and withdrawal of the product from the market by 29 April 2002.
On 16 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd met with the fourth defendant to instruct an immediate recall of Slim 10 and conducted random checks at retail outlets to verify compliance.
On 30 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd learned from a Health Sciences Authority press release that fenfluramine, a controlled substance, had been detected in Slim 10.
In May 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd refunded approximately $606,000 to the fourth defendant in relation to Slim 10.

In June 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd and the third defendant were charged with various offences relating to Slim 10; after representations, nine regulatory charges proceeded against the company, which pleaded guilty and was fined a total of $45,000, while charges against the third defendant were withdrawn.
In mid-2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd sent Slim 10 samples to the Chemistry Centre (Western Australia) for analysis and sought an opinion from Dr David Joyce on the constituents and their effects.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether HealthBiz Pte Ltd's activities in importing, testing, licensing, supplying, and recalling Slim 10, including its handling of regulatory requirements and product information, are factually connected to the plaintiff's consumption of Slim 10 and subsequent liver failure.
Party_Details.Rule: An importing and marketing company for a health product may be held responsible where it controls the product's entry into the market, interfaces with regulators, and supplies the product into the distribution chain that reaches the consumer, and where its compliance with testing, documentation, and recall processes is causation and liability.
Party_Details.Application: HealthBiz Pte Ltd, through the third defendant and its staff, obtained slimming capsules from the first defendant, arranged for laboratory testing, and secured import and wholesale licences and product listing from the Health Sciences Authority. It entered into a sole distributorship agreement with the fourth defendant and supplied Slim 10 capsules both in branded retail packaging and in unmarked vials and foil packets to the fifth defendant and others. The company undertook to ensure that Slim 10 would not contain poisons or synthetic substances and to submit batch test results, but imported unlabelled capsules marked as gifts without detailed import records. It responded to regulatory queries about capsule characteristics, commissioned additional tests, and engaged with the Health Sciences Authority over complaints and recalls, including a voluntary suspension and subsequent full recall. During this period, the plaintiff obtained Slim 10 capsules that originated from HealthBiz Pte Ltd's imports and consumed them in the weeks before her liver failure. The company's role in bringing Slim 10 into Singapore, arranging its testing and licensing, and supplying it into the distribution chain forms a direct factual link between its conduct and the plaintiff's exposure to the product.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined HealthBiz Pte Ltd's central role as importer and marketer of Slim 10. It found that the company controlled the flow of capsules from the Chinese manufacturer into Singapore, arranged for testing at Setsco and DAC, and obtained regulatory approvals from the Health Sciences Authority. The judge noted that HealthBiz gave an undertaking to ensure that Slim 10 contained no poisons or synthetic substances and to provide batch test results, yet imported unlabelled capsules by air parcel, marked as gifts, without customs declarations or proper import records. The company did not submit batch-specific declarations for each consignment and did not test imported batches for poisons before multiple sales to the distributor. The court considered the company's correspondence with the Health Sciences Authority about differences in capsule appearance and powder, its commissioning of further tests, and its responses to escalating consumer complaints and regulatory meetings in early April 2002. It found that HealthBiz initially agreed to a voluntary suspension and recall but then sought to appeal against the recall, relying on assurances from the manufacturer. The judge held that Slim 10 capsules consumed by the plaintiff were part of the product imported and supplied by HealthBiz, and that the company's failures in record-keeping, batch testing, and regulatory compliance contributed to unsafe product reaching consumers. On this basis, HealthBiz was held liable in negligence and under relevant regulatory frameworks for the plaintiff's injuries.

Party_Details.Name: Semon Liu Kelun

Facts.Text: Around 1992, the third defendant, Semon Liu Kelun, started his first business dealing with telecommunications equipment and later moved into food, pet shop, and indoor archery businesses.
In early 1999, Semon Liu Kelun weighed approximately 135 kg and was described as grossly overweight.
On a visit to Shanghai in early 1999, Semon Liu Kelun was introduced to a slimming capsule called 'yue zhi tang qing zhi shu' and purchased six bottles to try over two months.
After two months of taking the Shanghai slimming capsules, Semon Liu Kelun observed a weight loss of about 12 kg.
By July 1999, after further use of the slimming capsules, Semon Liu Kelun reported losing an additional 20 kg without experiencing side effects.
In 2000, Semon Liu Kelun purchased more of the slimming product for himself, family, and friends and took approximately thirty capsules a day, reducing his weight to about 69 kg by the end of 2000.
In January 2001, during a business trip to Guangzhou, Semon Liu Kelun was introduced by the Shanghai distributor to the manufacturer of the slimming product, later identified as the first defendant.
Semon Liu Kelun remained in Guangzhou for almost one month in January 2001 to discuss with the manufacturer his interest in distributing the slimming product in Singapore and other markets.
In March 2001, acting for HealthBiz Pte Ltd, Semon Liu Kelun ordered 200 bottles of the slimming capsules from the first defendant for product testing and consumption.
Around March 2001, Semon Liu Kelun approached DAC Pharmalab Pte Ltd to handle manufacturing, packaging, and storage of Slim 10 and to arrange microbiological and toxic heavy metal testing at Setsco.
Semon Liu Kelun chose the name 'Slim 10' for the slimming product, referencing the film '10' and the ten days consume 120 capsules at the recommended dosage.
In July 2001, Semon Liu Kelun attended the launch in Guangzhou of the first defendant's new slimming product 'yue zhi tang jian fei jiao nang', receiving a handbook and brochures.
In 2001, Semon Liu Kelun requested from the first defendant various documents including company licence, product testing results on animals and humans, and factory inspection certificates.

Through a MediaCorp contact, Semon Liu Kelun arranged a meeting with the fifth defendant and Chen Liping at a hotel, attended also by Tan Eng Kiat, to discuss Slim 10 endorsement and to provide them with product articles and Setsco test reports.
A few days after the hotel meeting, Chen Liping began taking sample Slim 10 pills provided by Semon Liu Kelun and reported losing about 10 kg within two weeks.

From 16 October 2001 onwards, Semon Liu Kelun or Peter Boo met the fifth defendant at convenient locations to hand over Slim 10 capsules in various forms, with the fifth defendant signing handwritten receipts.

In December 2001 and January 2002, Semon Liu Kelun frequently visited the fourth defendant's office to assist its call centre in handling customer complaints about Slim 10 side effects and to relay manufacturer feedback.

In early January 2002, after consulting the manufacturer, Semon Liu Kelun advised that customers experiencing side effects should reduce the Slim 10 dosage to two capsules three times a day and take them after food, leading to changes in dosage instructions on packaging in March 2002.

On or about 18 March 2002, when the fourth defendant observed an escalation in customer complaints, Semon Liu Kelun explained that herb potency varied by batch and suggested that reducing dosage would alleviate side effects.

On 2 April 2002, while in China, Semon Liu Kelun was informed by Peter Boo of a Health Sciences Authority meeting about Slim 10-related medical complaints and agreed to a voluntary two-week suspension and temporary recall.

On 3 April 2002, Semon Liu Kelun decided to appeal against the voluntary recall and, with assistance from the fourth defendant, approved a letter to the Health Sciences Authority seeking reconsideration and time for further investigations.
On 11 April 2002, HealthBiz Pte Ltd informed the fourth defendant that permission had been given to resume delivery of Slim 10 to retailers, with updated dosage instructions on boxes and vials.

On 16 April 2002, after receiving notice of an immediate recall from the Health Sciences Authority, Semon Liu Kelun met the fourth defendant to implement the recall and conducted random outlet checks.
On 30 April 2002, Semon Liu Kelun learned from a Health Sciences Authority press release that fenfluramine had been detected in Slim 10.

In June 2002, Semon Liu Kelun and HealthBiz Pte Ltd were charged with offences under the Medicines (Licensing, Standard Provisions and Fees) Regulations; the company pleaded guilty to nine charges and was fined, while charges against him personally were withdrawn.

On 29 August 2002, Semon Liu Kelun requested Keith Norman at the Chemistry Centre (Western Australia) to test Slim 10 samples for N-nitrosofenfluramine; he was informed the next day that the compound was detected in all samples.

After learning of the presence of N-nitrosofenfluramine in Slim 10 samples, Semon Liu Kelun was aware that Keith Norman notified the Australian Therapeutical Goods Administration and later published a report on the finding.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Semon Liu Kelun’s personal conduct in sourcing, testing, branding, and promoting Slim 10, and in managing regulatory interactions and supplies to the fifth defendant and the fourth defendant, is factually connected to the plaintiff’s consumption of Slim 10 and liver failure, and whether he bears personal responsibility distinct from HealthBiz Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: An individual behind a company may be personally responsible where he personally directs and controls the importation, formulation, branding, and distribution of a product that is factually linked to a consumer’s injury, and where his actions go beyond ordinary corporate oversight into direct involvement in the acts causing harm.
Party_Details.Application: Semon Liu Kelun personally used the Chinese slimming capsules extensively, experienced significant weight loss, and then initiated the Slim 10 business by negotiating directly with the Chinese manufacturer, arranging testing, and signing a distribution agreement on behalf of HealthBiz Pte Ltd. He chose the Slim 10 brand, negotiated the sole distributorship with the fourth defendant, and personally cultivated the endorsement relationship with the fifth defendant and Chen Liping, providing them with samples and test reports. He oversaw the importation of unlabelled capsules, the submission of regulatory applications, and the responses to Health Sciences Authority queries. He also coordinated with the fourth defendant on marketing, dosage changes, and handling of complaints, and supplied discounted Slim 10 to the fifth defendant for distribution to artistes, including the plaintiff. His decisions regarding product sourcing, testing, and recall directly shaped the availability and characteristics of Slim 10 consumed by the plaintiff. These facts his personal conduct was closely intertwined with the corporate acts that led to the plaintiff’s exposure to Slim 10.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered whether the third defendant, Semon Liu Kelun, should be personally liable in addition to HealthBiz Pte Ltd. It reviewed his extensive personal involvement in the Slim 10 venture: his prior use of the Chinese capsules, his negotiations with the manufacturer, his role in securing regulatory approvals, his choice of brand name, and his direct dealings with the fourth and fifth defendants. The judge noted that he arranged for capsules to be imported as unlabelled goods marked as gifts, without proper documentation or batch records, and that he did not ensure that each imported batch was tested for poisons despite the company’s undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority. The court also observed that he was the driving force behind the marketing strategy, including the use of a celebrity spokesperson and the supply of discounted capsules to artistes. Although criminal charges against him personally were withdrawn, the civil analysis focused on whether he had personally participated in the acts that caused harm. The court concluded that his actions went beyond passive directorship and that he was personally involved in the tortious conduct of importing and supplying a dangerous product, thereby incurring personal liability alongside the company.

Party_Details.Name: TV Media Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 1 March 2001, TV Media Pte Ltd entered into a sole distributor agreement with HealthBiz Pte Ltd to act as the sole distributor of Slim 10 in Singapore.

In 2001, TV Media's Regional Marketing Director, Wilfred Wong, and his subordinate met Semon Liu Kelun, who presented his business profile, photographs with celebrities and public figures, and vials of the slimming pills.
At subsequent meetings, Semon Liu Kelun brought a local celebrity who confirmed using the pills and provided three packets of pills in Ziploc bags for Wilfred Wong and two staff members to try.

During discussions, Semon Liu Kelun coined the name 'Slim 10' and proposed pricing at US$30.50 per vial, later increased to US$32, stating that each batch would be tested and that manufacturing and packing would occur in Singapore for quality control.

Semon Liu Kelun told TV Media representatives that the Chinese manufacturer was owned by Chen Guang Yu, whom he described as well respected in traditional Chinese medicine, and showed photographs of a modern factory without disclosing its exact identity or location.

Semon Liu Kelun assured TV Media that the herbs used in Slim 10 were of high quality, that the product had been on the Chinese market for about three years, and that he would obtain all necessary approvals from the Health Sciences Authority.
After Chen Liping was signed as the Slim 10 spokesperson, TV Media met her and the fifth defendant; Chen Liping began taking the pills in September 2001 and reported satisfactory weight loss results.
On 7 December 2001, the Health Sciences Authority approved TV Media's television commercial scripts for Slim 10.
On 16 December 2001, TV Media filmed the Slim 10 television commercial featuring Chen Liping, due to her busy schedule during filming of 'No Problem'.
On 28 December 2001, TV Media began airing 30-second Slim 10 television commercials, with longer versions broadcast from 24 January 2002.
Slim 10 packaging designed and distributed by TV Media displayed the list of ingredients on the grey cardboard box and on the transparent plastic vials, and included TV Media's contact number.
After Slim 10's launch, TV Media received favourable feedback from overseas contacts trying the product and handled customer complaints via its call centre, with assistance from Semon Liu Kelun.

When customers reported side effects such as headache, nausea, or heatiness, TV Media, following advice from Semon Liu Kelun, recommended reducing dosage and increasing water intake, after which some customers reported no further problems.
TV Media did not seek independent medical advice on Slim 10's ingredients, relying instead on the Health Sciences Authority's product listing approval and the ingredient list on the packaging.

On 18 March 2002, TV Media observed a marked increase in customer complaints about side effects; Semon Liu Kelun attributed this to higher herb potency in a recent batch and suggested dosage reduction.
By March 2002, TV Media and HealthBiz Pte Ltd were preparing a detailed Slim 10 information sheet for insertion into boxes, but this was not implemented due to events in early April 2002.

On 2 April 2002, at about 5.30 pm, Peter Boo informed TV Media of a Health Sciences Authority meeting about Slim 10-related medical complaints and showed a letter from HealthBiz Pte Ltd agreeing to a voluntary recall.

On the night of 2 April 2002, TV Media's Wilfred Wong assisted in drafting an appeal letter for HealthBiz Pte Ltd to send to the Health Sciences Authority, after faxing several drafts to Semon Liu Kelun in China for approval.

On 5 April 2002, Peter Boo informed TV Media that the Health Sciences Authority had instructed that Slim 10 not be distributed while the appeal was under consideration and that product testing would take about two weeks.

On 6 April 2002, Peter Boo told TV Media that HealthBiz Pte Ltd had decided to recall all Slim 10 batches delivered before 12 March 2002 due to old dosage instructions, and TV Media began the recall on 8 April 2002.
On 8 April 2002, a Health Sciences Authority inspector visited TV Media and took sample boxes from each of three Slim 10 batches delivered to TV Media.
On 11 April 2002, TV Media received a letter from HealthBiz Pte Ltd stating that permission had been given to resume delivery of Slim 10 to retailers, with updated dosage instructions on packaging.

On 16 April 2002, TV Media was informed that the Health Sciences Authority had ordered an immediate recall of Slim 10, partly because nicotinamide detected in the capsules was not listed among the ingredients.
On 24 April 2002, the Health Sciences Authority issued an update mentioning possible adverse reactions to Slim 10 but stating that direct causation had not yet been established.
On 26 April 2002, the Health Sciences Authority issued a second update advising consumers to stop taking Slim 10 as a precautionary measure.
On 30 April 2002, after the Health Sciences Authority announced that fenfluramine had been detected in Slim 10, TV Media organised a refund exercise for customers who had purchased the product.

During the refund exercise, TV Media received returns of capsules in packaging different from its marketed vials, including plain plastic bags, silver foil packets, and vials of different design, indicating that some consumers had obtained Slim 10 in non-standard packaging.

TV Media staff reported that a MediaCorp artiste, Sean Say, returned 206 capsules in a plain plastic bag, stating he had no proper vials, and other customers returned capsules of slightly different shade and size.
TV Media was aware that HealthBiz Pte Ltd supplied unmarked Slim 10 capsules in vials and foil packets to artistes, including the fifth defendant, as part of product testing and promotional efforts.

In March 2002, during an overseas business trip, Semon Liu Kelun told Wilfred Wong that he had difficulty collecting payment from the fifth defendant for Slim 10 sets supplied, revealing that substantial quantities had been provided to the fifth defendant.

TV Media did not apply for a wholesale dealer licence for Slim 10, relying on information from Peter Boo that HealthBiz Pte Ltd already held such a licence, and indicated its role as distributor on Slim 10 boxes approved by the Health Sciences Authority.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether TV Media Pte Ltd’s role as sole distributor and marketer of Slim 10, including its advertising, handling of complaints, and participation in recall and refund processes, is factually connected to the plaintiff’s consumption of Slim 10 and liver failure.
Party_Details.Rule: A distributor and marketer of a health product may be held responsible where it promotes the product, places it into retail channels, and participates in decisions about warnings, dosage instructions, and recalls, and where its conduct contributes to consumers’ exposure to the product that is factually linked to injury.
Party_Details.Application: TV Media Pte Ltd entered into a sole distributorship agreement with HealthBiz Pte Ltd, developed and aired television commercials featuring a well-known artiste, and distributed Slim 10 in branded packaging through retail outlets. It relied on the Health Sciences Authority’s product listing and the importer’s assurances about safety and testing, and did not seek independent medical advice. TV Media handled customer complaints, adjusted dosage instructions based on information from the importer, and coordinated recalls and appeals with HealthBiz Pte Ltd when adverse reactions were reported. The plaintiff saw TV Media’s Slim 10 commercials, observed in-store promotional materials at TV Media’s retail outlet, and associated the product’s safety with TV Media’s corporate reputation. Although the plaintiff purchased Slim 10 through the fifth defendant rather than directly from retail outlets, the capsules she consumed were part of the same product line distributed by TV Media, and her decision to use Slim 10 was influenced by TV Media’s advertising and branding. TV Media’s actions in marketing and distributing Slim 10 therefore form part of the factual chain leading to the plaintiff’s consumption and subsequent liver failure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court assessed TV Media’s liability as the sole distributor and promoter of Slim 10. It found that TV Media played a key role in creating consumer demand through high-profile advertising featuring Chen Liping and in presenting Slim 10 as a safe, herbal product. The judge noted that the plaintiff’s interest in Slim 10 was sparked by TV Media’s commercials and in-store displays, and that she relied on TV Media’s corporate reputation rather than on the identities of the importer or manufacturer. The court considered TV Media’s reliance on the Health Sciences Authority’s product listing and the importer’s assurances, but held that this did not absolve it from responsibility for promoting and distributing a product that contained undeclared substances. The court also examined TV Media’s involvement in drafting the appeal letter against the voluntary recall, its handling of complaints, and its participation in the recall and refund processes. It concluded that TV Media’s conduct contributed to the widespread distribution and aggressive promotion of Slim 10, which in turn led to the plaintiff’s consumption of the product. Accordingly, TV Media was held liable in negligence alongside the importer and manufacturer.

Party_Details.Name: Rayson Tan Tai Ming

Facts.Text: By the material time, the fifth defendant, Rayson Tan Tai Ming, was married to Chen Liping for about five years and they had a one-year-old son.
Before the Slim 10 endorsement, the fifth defendant and his wife did not know Semon Liu Kelun until he approached them regarding Slim 10.

At their first meeting in 2001, Semon Liu Kelun showed the fifth defendant and Chen Liping his portfolio and told them he had taken the slimming pills he planned to import for about two years, describing them as primarily plant-based and herbal and explaining the regulatory process.
The fifth defendant and Chen Liping believed that if the Health Sciences Authority approved the product and MediaCorp agreed to an endorsement, the pills would be safe for sale.

Around July 2001, at Semon Liu Kelun’s request, Chen Liping began taking Slim 10 pills before formal approval, and over about two months experienced significant weight loss, leading friends and relatives to ask her about the pills.
By November 2001, Slim 10 newspaper advertisements featuring Chen Liping had been published, followed by television commercials, increasing public awareness of her weight loss.
As a result of Chen Liping’s weight loss, many close friends and relatives approached her to help them purchase Slim 10 pills, and she agreed to do so.
Semon Liu Kelun told Chen Liping he was prepared to sell Slim 10 at a discount to their close friends and relatives in appreciation of their trust.

In December 2001, Chen Liping became pregnant and she and the fifth defendant consulted her gynaecologist about Slim 10, showing the packaging; they were told the pills should not affect the pregnancy.

Due to Chen Liping’s pregnancy, the fifth defendant began running errands by collecting Slim 10 from Semon Liu Kelun and delivering them to friends and relatives, paying initially in cash and later by cash cheques and cash.

From 3 December 2001 to 11 February 2002, the fifth defendant and the plaintiff were cast as husband and wife in the Chinese drama 'No Problem', working closely together as main leads and becoming good friends.

During the filming period, the plaintiff and the fifth defendant exchanged SMS messages in their free time, and the plaintiff gave him a Christmas gift, to which he reciprocated with a gift chosen with Chen Liping’s help.
The plaintiff shared personal information with the fifth defendant during filming, including details of her MediaCorp earnings.

In late December 2001, during a casual conversation on set, the plaintiff remarked on Chen Liping’s weight loss and asked the fifth defendant about the secret; he explained that she had been taking Slim 10 and repeated what he and his wife had been told about the pills’ composition.
In the same conversation, the plaintiff asked about the price of Slim 10, and the fifth defendant told her that the Slim 10 boss was giving a discount to their friends and relatives.

In a subsequent conversation, the plaintiff asked the fifth defendant to help her buy Slim 10 from the boss; he initially advised against it because she was already slim, but she persisted and he agreed to help as a favour, with no specific payment or delivery date discussed.
On or about 11 January 2002, the fifth defendant collected two sets of 120 Slim 10 capsules from Semon Liu Kelun and handed them to the plaintiff at the studio; she paid him $260 in cash.

At the time of the first handover, Chen Liping told the plaintiff she was fine taking the pills, that there was no need to diet, and advised her to continue regular meals and drink plenty of water while taking Slim 10.

Around January 2002, the fifth defendant used a birthday discount from GNC to purchase Biotin shampoo for the plaintiff at her request, paying in advance and asking her to collect the shampoo from the outlet and reimburse him.

On 15 February 2002, the plaintiff sent the fifth defendant an SMS stating she had run out of 'skinny pills' and asking if he would be at MediaCorp that day; this was followed by a phone call requesting more Slim 10.

Following the 15 February 2002 request, the fifth defendant obtained two more sets of Slim 10 from Semon Liu Kelun, placed them in the plaintiff’s pigeonhole at work, and informed her by SMS; she left a cheque for $260 dated 18 February 2002 in his pigeonhole.

Around 21 March 2002, the fifth defendant received another SMS and a phone call from the plaintiff asking him to buy two bottles of Slim 10 for her; he initially discouraged her but she said she wanted them for maintenance during a Bangkok holiday, and he agreed.

For the third batch, the fifth defendant collected two bottles of Slim 10 from Semon Liu Kelun and left them in the plaintiff’s pigeonhole; the plaintiff collected them but did not pay before her hospitalisation.

In April 2002, while hospitalised, the plaintiff telephoned the fifth defendant and asked if she could return the third batch of two bottles of Slim 10 since she had not paid for them and had not opened them; he told her to focus on her recovery and discuss it later.

During the plaintiff’s first week at Mount Alvernia Hospital, the fifth defendant visited her and spoke with Pierre Png outside her room about her condition and possible assistance from the Slim 10 boss.
On 8 March 2002, the fifth defendant received 100 sets of Slim 10 from HealthBiz Pte Ltd, and on 9 March 2002 he issued a cash cheque for $10,000 as payment for these sets.
The fifth defendant asked some colleagues not to publicise that he could obtain Slim 10 for them, stating that it was troublesome and that he could not supply everyone.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the fifth defendant’s conduct in obtaining discounted Slim 10 from the importer and supplying it in unmarked containers to the plaintiff and other colleagues, and his communications about the product’s effects and side effects, is factually connected to the plaintiff’s consumption of Slim 10 and liver failure, and whether he bears responsibility as a supplier rather than a mere intermediary.
Party_Details.Rule: An individual who procures a product from a supplier and passes it on to others, receiving payment and facilitating repeated transactions, may be treated as a supplier for purposes of liability where his conduct forms a link in the chain by which the product reaches the consumer and contributes to the consumer’s decision to use it.
Party_Details.Application: The fifth defendant was closely connected to Slim 10 through his wife’s endorsement and his own interactions with the importer. He obtained Slim 10 at a discounted price from HealthBiz Pte Ltd and supplied it to friends, relatives, and colleagues, including the plaintiff, often in unmarked vials or foil packets. He received payments from the plaintiff for the first two batches and arranged a third batch that she collected but did not pay for before falling ill. He relayed information about the pills’ herbal nature and side effects, and his association with the Slim 10 spokesperson and the importer gave his assurances weight. The plaintiff chose to buy Slim 10 through him for privacy, convenience, and discount reasons, and she relied on his role as the husband of the poster girl and as a trusted colleague. His repeated procurement and delivery of Slim 10 to the plaintiff directly enabled her consumption of the capsules in the weeks before her liver failure. These facts he functioned as a supplier in the distribution chain rather than as a one-off messenger.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court evaluated whether the fifth defendant was merely doing favours or was effectively a seller of Slim 10. It considered his evidence that he acted out of friendship and did not profit, but contrasted this with the volume of Slim 10 he obtained from HealthBiz Pte Ltd, the handwritten receipts showing 950 sets, and the $10,000 cash cheque for 100 sets. The judge found that he regularly procured Slim 10 at a discount and passed it on to colleagues, including the plaintiff, receiving payment and controlling the terms of supply. The court noted that the plaintiff’s decision to use Slim 10 was influenced by his role as the husband of the poster girl and by his assurances about the pills, and that he supplied the capsules in unmarked containers that bypassed normal retail packaging and oversight. On these facts, the court rejected his characterisation of the transactions as mere favours and held that he was a supplier in the chain of distribution. Combined with the finding that Slim 10 caused the plaintiff’s liver failure, this led to a conclusion of liability against him alongside the corporate defendants.
